title: Rejected promise is cached permanently, making all future loadFixture calls for that id fail without retry
description: The promise `p` is inserted into `cache` before it resolves or rejects (line 90). If the async body throws (file missing, SHA-256 mismatch, fetch error), the rejected promise stays in the map. Every subsequent `loadFixture(id)` call hits the cached rejection and immediately rejects — there is no way to recover without restarting the process. This is especially problematic for ENOENT errors where the user is told to regenerate fixtures: after running the generator they cannot reload without a restart.

title: fetchLargeFixture has no AbortController/timeout, so a stalled remote fetch hangs indefinitely
description: The `fetch(fixture.url)` call (line 106) has no `signal` from an `AbortController` and no timeout. Large fixture downloads from slow or unreliable CDNs can stall indefinitely, blocking the test runner or any consumer waiting on the returned promise. There is no backoff or retry either. A standard pattern is to pass `signal: AbortSignal.timeout(30_000)` or create an explicit `AbortController` with `setTimeout`.
code_excerpt:
const response = await fetch(fixture.url);
  if (!response.ok) throw new Error(`Failed to fetch large fixture: ${id} (status: ${response.status})`);

title: Bare catch {} silently discards access() errors other than ENOENT, hiding permission or I/O failures
description: The `catch {}` on line 91 swallows every error from `fs.access()` unconditionally. While ENOENT is the expected case (file simply not present), errors such as EACCES (permission denied), ENXIO (device error), or transient I/O failures are silently ignored and the source file is skipped. If a valid file is inaccessible due to a permissions issue the user gets no diagnostic — the generator silently continues with fewer sources and may throw the generic 'No PGO source RAWs found' error without indicating which file was problematic.

category: missing error context / unhelpful error message
title: decodeRawToRgba called without try/catch — decode failure for one source aborts the entire fixture generation run without identifying which file failed
description: Inside the `for (const source of picked)` loop, `decodeRawToRgba(source, ...)` is called on line 178 with no error handling. If a source file is corrupt, unsupported, or partially written, the thrown error propagates out of `generatePgoFixtures` with a generic WASM/Rust message that does not include the offending filename. Wrapping the per-source block in try/catch and rethrowing with `source` appended would make failures actionable. Additionally, all directories were already cleared (line 166-167) before the loop, so a mid-loop failure leaves the output in a partially-written state with no recovery.

title: renderSrgb8 and sibling render functions divide by (width-1) and (height-1), producing NaN for 1×N or N×1 images
description: All five render helper functions (renderSrgb8, renderSrgbAlpha8, renderAdobeRgb16, renderGrayRamp16, renderSaturatedGreen16) use `x / (width - 1)` and `y / (height - 1)` as gradient fractions. When `width === 1` or `height === 1` the divisor is 0, `Math.round(NaN)` returns `NaN`, and `Uint8Array/Uint16Array` coerces `NaN` to `0`, silently producing an all-black image. The fixture generator currently uses `width = height = 100` so this does not trigger in practice, but any future caller with a 1-pixel dimension will get a silently wrong output with no error.

title: loadFixture rejection is not awaited with await in the test — missing await means the assertion may not catch the rejection correctly in all test runners
description: The test on line 30 calls `expect(loadFixture("non-existent")).rejects.toThrow(...)` without `await`. In Bun's test runner this pattern works because Bun tracks the returned promise from `rejects.toThrow()`, but in Jest or Vitest the missing `await` means the assertion promise is not awaited and the test can pass vacuously even if the rejection does not happen. This is a fragile pattern and a common source of false-passing tests when migrating test runners.

title: Loader throws plain Error strings — no typed error classes make catch-site handling and test assertions brittle
description: All error paths in `loadFixture` and `fetchLargeFixture` throw `new Error(string)`. Callers that want to distinguish 'fixture not found' from 'SHA-256 mismatch' from 'network failure' must parse the message string, which is fragile. Defining typed error classes (e.g., `FixtureNotFoundError`, `FixtureHashMismatchError`, `FixtureFetchError`) would allow callers to branch on `instanceof`, improve test assertions, and make error messages stable across refactors.

category: missing error handling on reader stream
title: ReadableStream reader.read() loop in fetchLargeFixture has no try/finally to cancel the reader on error, leaking the stream lock
description: The streaming read loop (lines 118-126) calls `reader.read()` without a try/finally block. If an error is thrown mid-loop (e.g., a network interruption causes `reader.read()` to reject), the `ReadableStreamDefaultReader` lock is never released via `reader.cancel()` or `reader.releaseLock()`. The locked stream cannot be garbage-collected until the page unloads, and any caller that holds the original response body reference will be unable to reuse or cancel it. Wrapping the loop in try/finally with `reader.cancel()` in the finally block fixes this.
